---
title: "January 20th, 2026"
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

## Summary

NASA's Ice, Cloud, and Land Elevation Satellite (ICESat) launched in 2002 and collected data using its Geoscience Laser Altimeter System (GLAS) instrumentation between 2003 and 2010, when it ceased functioning. Its successor, ICESat-2, has replaced it since 2018. Fundamentally, both measure the distance from the satellite instrumentation to the Earth's surface by shooting pulses of laser light at the Earth and recording the time required for the reflection of the laser to reach a telescope mounted parallel to the laser. On-board processing is used to detect the pulses approximately and select the regions of data to transmit to the ground for more refined processing. ICESat could only handle 40 pulses per second, but ICESat-2 can do 10000. Consequently, ICESat-2 collects 250 times more points along the same path length. The original could measure with about 10 cm resolution, and the current resolution is about 5 mm.

``` {r echo=FALSE}
library(xaringan)
library(xaringanExtra)
xaringanExtra::embed_xaringan(url = "https://sam-mize.github.io/casa0023/slideshow.html#1",
                              ratio = "16:9")
```

## Applications

Although ICESat and ICESat-2 were primarily intended to monitor ice in polar regions, many other uses have been found for the measurements they collected. Accuracy is typically much lower for snow depth measurements in mountainous areas further from the poles, because the passes are further apart and because the steepness of the terrain and presence of vegetation interferes with the reflection of light from the surface, but specialized filtering and aggregation methods applied to ICESat-2 data have improved accuracy in Western US states  [@noauthor_mountain_2024]. Accuracy relative to *in situ* measurement is still limited by the accuracy of the model of the underlying terrain, which is still somewhat affected by those same issues of steepness and vegetation even when it's measured by aerial LiDAR. @sunDiscoveringForestHeight2020 found that combining data across both ICESat missions enabled them to estimate reforestation at low (1km) resolution in remote area of China, which is useful for carbon balance estimations and general environmental monitoring, but again required specialized data processing and aggregation to overcome limitations (especially those of the original ICESat) and *in situ* measurements to compare against. 

## Reflection

I have used similar devices (in principle, not precision or expense) to measure distances in my house for hanging wall art and various renovation projects, and have directly experienced the difficulty of achieving an accurate measurement on a sloped surface. Especially when you're measuring a concave corner and the paint is glossy enough, you sometimes get a bit of a double-bounce. I can easily see how having one in space would be useful but tricky to employ. Snow is extremely important to the Western US, since water is often otherwise scarce there. And, for some reason, they don't always get as much snow as they used to. Accurate monitoring should help those States better ration the use of their limited resource. The higher precision and resolution of ICESat-2 has enabled more use outside the primary ice-tracking goal of the mission. Maybe ICESat-3 with bring another 250-fold increase in resolution soon.
